primary_diagnosis.name: Q fever fatigue syndrome (post-infectious/post-viral fatigue following Chronic Q Fever, C. burnetii) — acute post-exertional exacerbation

comorbidities.name: Past acute Q fever (C. burnetii) infection ~2 years ago

pathophysiology_summary: This is an acute post-exertional exacerbation ('crash') of an established post-infectious fatigue syndrome that developed after documented acute Q fever (Coxiella burnetii) two years earlier — Q fever fatigue syndrome (QFS), a recognised chronic post-Q-fever sequela. The defining mechanism is post-exertional malaise: exertion two days prior triggered a disproportionate, prolonged, sleep-unrefreshed collapse in energy with diffuse myalgia and cognitive slowing ('brain fog', slowed speech). The presentation is deliberately alarming and superficially mimics emergencies, but the discriminating features are reassuring and largely patient-obtainable: the deficit is symmetric and global (not focal/lateralising), onset is over two days with a clear exertional trigger (not sudden), the patient is afebrile with normal home vitals and normal peripheral perfusion, and the pattern matches prior recurrent crashes. The two genuinely dangerous mimics — acute ischaemic stroke and chronic Q fever endocarditis — were excluded in-person by a normal neurological examination (intact cranial nerves, symmetric tone/reflexes, no focal deficit) and normal cardiac auscultation (no new murmur), neither of which telehealth can perform; hence the correct telehealth posture is presumptive recognition of the QFS flare PLUS conservative, explicit safety-netting for those mimics, not reflexive over-escalation. The principal acute risks are dehydration (too exhausted to self-hydrate) and, longer-term, unrecognised chronic Q fever endocarditis (an echocardiogram and ID review belong in longitudinal care).

differential.diagnosis: Q fever fatigue syndrome — post-exertional exacerbation
differential.evidence_basis: Known 2-year post-Q-fever fatigue illness with recurrent crashes; current crash follows clear over-exertion — classic post-exertional malaise pattern.
New cognitive slowing, slowed speech and inability to stand must prompt active exclusion of an acute focal neurological event, especially as telehealth cannot examine.
Prior C. burnetii infection carries a real risk of chronic Q fever endocarditis presenting with fatigue/malaise — must be screened (fever, sweats, new murmur, breathlessness).
Profound lethargy with reduced oral intake could reflect or be worsened by dehydration/electrolyte derangement.
Profound low energy and withdrawal could suggest severe depression; requires consideration but the physiological exertional trigger and objective myalgia argue against it as the primary.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the outset the AI Doctor should recognise the likely known-condition flare while explicitly listing stroke and chronic Q fever endocarditis as important-not-to-miss mimics that need active screening — NOT jump straight to emergency escalation, and NOT dismiss the serious mimics.

differential.diagnosis: Q fever fatigue syndrome — post-exertional exacerbation
differential.evidence_basis: Symmetric global picture with clear exertional trigger, sleep-unrefreshed, afebrile with normal home vitals, recurrent recognised pattern — coheres as a QFS crash.

Symmetric, global deficit with no unilateral facial droop, no lateralising weakness, non-slurred speech and a 2-day exertional build argue strongly against an acute focal stroke — though definitive exclusion needs in-person exam, so it remains a safety-net trigger.

Afebrile with no new breathlessness or chest pain lowers acute probability, but chronic Q fever endocarditis cannot be excluded remotely — echocardiogram and ID review remain part of longitudinal care.
Reduced intake and low urine output make dehydration a real contributor and an active management target and safety-net trigger.
The clear physical over-exertion trigger, objective diffuse myalgia and sleep-unrefreshed physiological crash argue against a primary depressive episode as the driver of this acute presentation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After screening, stroke and depression are correctly downgraded/excluded on available data; QFS flare is leading; chronic Q fever endocarditis and dehydration remain live for safety-netting and longitudinal work-up. The AI Doctor should now be moving toward an outpatient disposition with robust safety-netting, not ED transport.

differential.diagnosis: Q fever fatigue syndrome — post-exertional exacerbation
differential.evidence_basis: Best-fitting, recognised flare of the known chronic condition; manage supportively at home with prompt review and conservative safety-netting for the mimics telehealth cannot fully exclude.
Not the current driver but a lethal complication of prior Q fever that must be excluded in longitudinal care (echo + ID review) and safety-netted for (fever, sweats, breathlessness).

differential_progression.scoring_note: The only correct final telehealth disposition is outpatient recognition-and-support of the QFS crash (~T2) with prominent, specific safety-netting; reflexive ED/000 escalation of this screened-negative flare is the scored error, as is false reassurance that omits safety-netting.

red_flags.description: New focal / lateralising neurological deficit — unilateral facial droop, one-sided limb weakness, slurred speech, sudden severe headache, or vision loss (acute stroke).
red_flags.clinical_significance: A focal deficit would upgrade to a time-critical stroke pathway. Its ABSENCE (symmetric, global, non-slurred picture) is the key discriminator that permits an outpatient disposition; if it appears, escalate to T5 immediately.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If a true evolving stroke were mislabelled as a fatigue crash, thrombolysis/thrombectomy windows would be missed — hence the mandatory focal-deficit safety-net.

red_flags.description: Fever, rigors, drenching night sweats, new breathlessness, or new cardiac murmur — possible acute infection relapse or chronic Q fever (C. burnetii) endocarditis.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Prior Q fever carries a real risk of chronic Q fever endocarditis, a lethal complication that can present with fatigue and malaise. Currently afebrile with no cardiorespiratory features; must be safety-netted and worked up (echo, ID review) in longitudinal care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated chronic Q fever endocarditis is progressive and can be fatal without prolonged antimicrobial therapy and cardiology/ID management.

red_flags.description: Reduced conscious level — becoming unrousable or unresponsive to voice, pain, or shaking; or shallow/erratic breathing.
red_flags.clinical_significance: The patient is profoundly lethargic but currently rousable with calm regular breathing. A drop to unrousable or respiratory compromise is an immediate life threat requiring 000.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A deteriorating conscious level or respiratory failure left unrecognised at home could be fatal.

red_flags.description: Progressive dehydration — unable to keep any fluids down, not passing urine, increasing confusion/drowsiness, dizziness on sitting, very dry mouth.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Markedly reduced oral intake with low urine output on a background of total dependence makes dehydration an active acute risk and a management target; if it progresses it warrants urgent same-day in-person assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated dehydration/electrolyte disturbance causes acute kidney injury, worsening confusion and hospitalisation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The dramatic presentation (near-collapse, cannot stand, cognitive failure, 'urgent home visit' framing) pulls toward reflexive ED/000 escalation, ignoring that the acute red-flag screen is negative and that transport itself would catastrophically worsen this patient's post-exertional crash.

Using 'I can't examine you, so you must go to ED to be safe' as a default. Here the discriminating data (symmetric/global deficit, afebrile, normal home vitals, known recurrent pattern) are patient-obtainable and reassuring; the correct response is presumptive recognition plus conservative safety-netting, not blanket escalation.

The opposite failure — anchoring on the known fatigue label and dismissing the case ('it's just your fatigue, rest up') without actively screening for and safety-netting the stroke, endocarditis and dehydration mimics.
Attributing the presentation to depression and missing the physiological post-exertional pattern and objective diffuse myalgia.
Being drawn by the source's supplement regimen (low-dose naltrexone, NAD+, zinc, vitamin C) into synthesising doses or endorsing them as definitive acute treatment despite weak/absent evidence.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Neurological examination — cranial nerves, tone, power, reflexes and coordination to definitively exclude an acute focal neurological event
Cardiac auscultation — new/changed murmur to help exclude chronic Q fever endocarditis
Deep-muscle palpation for the diffuse tenderness across major compartments
Formal hydration/perfusion assessment and bloods (electrolytes, renal function, inflammatory markers)
Echocardiogram and Q fever (C. burnetii) phase I/II serology for chronic Q fever endocarditis work-up
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the acute post-exertional flare of the known post-infectious fatigue condition and keep the patient at home with radical rest and carer-assisted hydration (in-person transport is contraindicated during the crash), arrange prompt telehealth/GP review and longitudinal work-up (bloods, echocardiogram, ID review to exclude chronic Q fever endocarditis), and give prominent, specific, time-bound safety-netting for the serious mimics (stroke signs, reduced consciousness/breathing, new fever, progressive dehydration) with 000 for any of those.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two opposite harms: (1) FALSE REASSURANCE — dismissing as 'just your fatigue' and missing an evolving stroke, chronic Q fever endocarditis or dangerous dehydration; and (2) HARMFUL OVER-TRIAGE — reflexively sending this screened-negative flare to ED/000, where the journey and stimulation worsen the metabolic/sensory exhaustion. The correct posture is presumptive recognition PLUS conservative safety-netting.
